revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''梅木遺跡'''（うめのきいせき）は、[[群馬県]][[前橋市]]にある[[古墳時代]]の豪族居館遺跡。 [[赤城山]]の南麓、大...'
revision.text:
'''梅木遺跡'''（うめのきいせき）は、[[群馬県]][[前橋市]]にある[[古墳時代]]の豪族居館遺跡。
[[赤城山]]の南麓、[[大室公園]]内にある。館は堀と柵列で方形に区画されていたと思われるが、主要な施設は付近を流れる桂川のたびたびの氾濫により削平されほとんど残っていない。西南側からは張り出しと堀の広がりがあり、柱穴が検出されていることから門があったと考えられる。 堀の一辺は外側約85メートル、内側約65メートル、面積約6500平方メートル前後の正方形と推定される。 堀は古墳時代中期末の竪穴式住居を壊して作られ、また堀の底から6世紀初頭に榛名山から噴出した火山灰(Hr-FA)が堆積していることから、この館は5世紀後半に作られ、6世紀初頭まで使われたと考えられる。 この遺跡から西南へ400メートル行くと6世紀初頭の築造と推定されている[[前二子古墳]]があり、この遺跡との関連がうかがわれる。
== 参考文献 == *前原豊 『東国大豪族の威勢・大室古墳群〔群馬〕』 シリーズ「遺跡を学ぶ」063 新泉社 2009年
{{DEFAULTSORT:うめのきいせき}} [[Category:日本の考古遺跡]] [[Category:前橋市の歴史]] [[Category:古墳時代]]

'''梅木遺跡'''（うめのきいせき）は、[[群馬県]][[前橋市]]にある[[古墳時代]]の豪族居館遺跡。
[[赤城山]]の南麓、[[大室公園]]内にある。館は堀と柵列で方形に区画されていたと思われるが、主要な施設は付近を流れる桂川のたびたびの氾濫により削平されほとんど残っていない。西南側からは張り出しと堀の広がりがあり、柱穴が検出されていることから門があったと考えられる。
堀の一辺は外側約85メートル、内側約65メートル、面積約6500平方メートル前後の正方形と推定される。 堀は古墳時代中期末の竪穴式住居を壊して作られ、また堀の底から6世紀初頭に榛名山から噴出した火山灰(Hr-FA)が堆積していることから、この館は5世紀後半に作られ、6世紀初頭まで使われたと考えられる。
この遺跡から西南へ400メートル行くと6世紀初頭の築造と推定されている[[前二子古墳]]があり、この遺跡との関連がうかがわれる。
== 参考文献 == *前原豊 『東国大豪族の威勢・大室古墳群〔群馬〕』 シリーズ「遺跡を学ぶ」063 新泉社 2009年

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:梅木遺跡]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q17217678]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{混同|Umenoki Site (Yamanashi)|x1=located in Yamanashi Prefecture}}
[[File:Umenoki Site (Maebashi), gaikan.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{left|View of the Umenoki Site, with [[:en:Mount Akagi|Mount Akagi]] in the background}]]]
{{Location map|Japan Gunma Prefecture| lat_deg = 36| lat_min = 23| lat_sec = 14.9| lon_deg = 139| lon_min = 12| lon_sec = 05.6| label = Umenoki Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 300|relief=1| caption={{left|Location map.}}}}

'''Umenoki Site''' (梅木遺跡, Umenoki Iseki) is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] in [[Nishi-Ōmuro-chō]], [[Maebashi City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], Japan, featuring remains of a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:chieftain|chieftain]]’s residence.

== Overview ==
The site is located at the southern base of [[:en:Mount Akagi|Mount Akagi]], within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA [[:en:tephra|tephra]]) from the early 6th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]] found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the [[:en:Maefutago_Kofun|Mae-Futago Kofun]], a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

== References ==

{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
{{small|Sources used for the article:}}
* Maehara Yutaka, ''The Prestige of Eastern Japan’s Great Clans: The Ōmuro Kofuns [Gunma]'', Series "Learning from Archaeological Sites" 063, Shinsensha, 2009.
*{{Cite book|和書|author=Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|title=Ōmuro Kofuns in Maebashi City|year=2016|date=2016-06-11|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/material/files/group/94/oomuro.pdf|publisher=Maebashi City Board of Education|ref=harv}}

== Related Literature ==
{{small|Additional resources not used in this article:}}
* {{Cite book|和書|editor=Yamabe Archaeological Research Institute|author=|year=1986|chapter=|title=[https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/15481 Umenoki Site]|publisher=Maebashi City Excavation Team|isbn=|ref=}} - Link provided by the Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties' "National Archaeological Report Database."
== See Also ==

The site is located at the southern base of [[:en:Mount Akagi|Mount Akagi]], within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA [[:en:tephra|tephra]]) from the early 6th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]] found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the [[:en:Maefutago_Kofun|Mae-Futago Kofun]], a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

{{Reflist}}

*{{Cite book|和書|author=Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|title=Ōmuro Kofuns in Maebashi City|year=2016|date=2016-06-11|url=https://www.city.maebashi.gunma.jp/material/files/group/94/oomuro.pdf|publisher=Maebashi City Board of Education|ref=harv}}

'''Umenoki Site''' (梅木遺跡, Umenoki Iseki) is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] in [[Nishi-Ōmuro-chō]], [[Maebashi City]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], Japan, featuring remains of a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:chieftain|chieftain]]’s residence.

== Overview ==
The site is located at the southern base of [[:en:Mount Akagi|Mount Akagi]], within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA [[:en:tephra|tephra]]) from the early 6th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]] found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the [[:en:Maefutago_Kofun|Mae-Futago Kofun]], a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

Volcanic ash (Hr-FA [[:en:tephra|tephra]]) from the early 6th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]] found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the [[:en:Maefutago_Kofun|Mae-Futago Kofun]], a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

== Overview ==
The site is located at the southern base of [[:en:Mount Akagi|Mount Akagi]], within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA [[:en:tephra|tephra]]) from the early 6th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]] found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the [[:en:Maefutago_Kofun|Mae-Futago Kofun]], a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

{{Location map|Japan Gunma Prefecture| lat_deg = 36| lat_min = 23| lat_sec = 14.9| lon_deg = 139| lon_min = 12| lon_sec = 05.6| label = Umenoki Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 300|relief=1| caption={{left|Location map.}}}}

'''Umenoki Site''' (梅木遺跡, Umenoki Iseki) is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site}} in [[Nishi-Ōmuro-chō]], [[Maebashi City]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, Japan, featuring remains of a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:chieftain|chieftain]]’s residence.

== Overview ==
The site is located at the southern base of {{ill|Mount Akagi|en|Mount Akagi|WD=Q415251|lt=Mount Akagi}}, within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA {{ill|tephra|en|tephra|WD=Q209245|lt=tephra}}) from the early 6th-century eruption of {{ill|Mount Haruna|en|Mount Haruna|WD=Q1191690|lt=Mount Haruna}} found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the {{ill|Maefutago_Kofun|en|Maefutago_Kofun|WD=Q17221412|lt=Mae-Futago Kofun}}, a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.

'''Umenoki Site''' (梅木遺跡, Umenoki Iseki) is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site|1=Archaeological site}} in [[Nishi-Ōmuro-chō]], [[Maebashi City]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, Japan, featuring remains of a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:chieftain|chieftain]]’s residence.

== Overview ==
The site is located at the southern base of {{ill|Mount Akagi|en|Mount Akagi|WD=Q415251|lt=Mount Akagi|1=Mount Akagi}}, within the grounds of [[Ōmuro Park]]. It is believed the residence was rectangular and enclosed by moats and fences. However, major features were eroded by repeated flooding of the nearby Katsura River, leaving only fragments. Excavations have revealed pillar holes and an expanded moat structure at the southwestern corner, suggesting the presence of a gate.

The moat is estimated to have measured about 85 meters on its outer edge, 65 meters on its inner edge, and encompassed an area of approximately 6,500 square meters. The moat was constructed over a mid-Kofun period [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]. Volcanic ash (Hr-FA {{ill|tephra|en|tephra|WD=Q209245|lt=tephra|1=Tephra}}) from the early 6th-century eruption of {{ill|Mount Haruna|en|Mount Haruna|WD=Q1191690|lt=Mount Haruna|1=Mount Haruna}} found at the bottom of the moat indicates the residence was built in the late 5th century and used until the early 6th century.{{Sfn|Maebashi City Board of Education, Cultural Heritage Division|2016|p=19}}

Approximately 400 meters southwest of this site lies the {{ill|Maefutago_Kofun|en|Maefutago_Kofun|WD=Q17221412|lt=Mae-Futago Kofun|1=Maefutago Kofun}}, a keyhole-shaped tomb from the early 6th century, which suggests a potential connection to the Umenoki Site.
